textContent:
Latinx
(lit from gender studies, linguistics, sociolinguistics)
   Etymology 
    - as a neologist term
    - 3rd grammatical gender
         - as neutral encompassing classifier
   Comparison 
    - Mx
   History 
   - latino/latina - blah blah - latinx
Pano naging halos magkaparehas yung mga Pilipino sa Latino
Filipinx
  - Parallelism sa latinx
  - Bakit kailangan ng bagong moniker
  - 

Both Latin Americans and Filipinos have been colonized by the Spaniards and has been affected by them linguistically, culturally, and spiritually. Though Filipinos may not speak Spanish, 333 years of contact has caused *word borrowing* from Spanish that the Phillipine languages developed a "pseudogrammatical" noun class system with the "-o" ending depicting the masculine gender and the "-a" ending the feminine. Both parties also share a commonality from their christianization and have the same LGBT discourse rooting from the 

'Filipinx' is another neologist term from the suffixation of the neogrammatical gender -x in parallel to the derivation of the term 'Latinx'. It also strives to erase the binary gender system imposed by the Spanish influence in the Philippine languages. The need for a more inclusive national identity moniker signifies for a country that is not just LGBT-tolerant but also LGBT-accepting. 

Ros Carl justin
title: How is the repudiation of 'Latinx' as identity moniker manifest the gender insensitivity of 2nd-gen Fil. Americans?

Both Latin Americans and Filipinos have been colonized by the Spaniards and has been affected by them linguistically, culturally, and spiritually. Though Filipinos may not speak Spanish, 333 years of contact has caused *word borrowing* from Spanish that the Phillipine languages developed a "pseudogrammatical" noun class system with the "-o" ending depicting the masculine gender and the "-a" ending the feminine. Both parties also share a commonality from their christianization and have the same LGBT discourse rooting from the 


'Filipinx' is another neologist term from the suffixation of the neogrammatical gender -x in parallel to the derivation of the term 'Latinx'. It also strives to erase the binary gender system imposed by the Spanish influence in the Philippine languages. The need for a more inclusive national identity moniker signifies for a country that is not just LGBT-tolerant but also LGBT-accepting.
